items.q: What are the four Ps of the marketing mix?
items.correct: Product, Price, Place (distribution), and Promotion — the controllable variables a business uses to satisfy customers and achieve objectives.
items.misconception: The four Ps are Product, Price, Profit, and People.
items.random_wrong: The four Ps are Planning, Producing, Promoting, and Profit.
items.why: Students often substitute 'profit' or 'people' into the four Ps. The classic McCarthy framework is Product, Price, Place and Promotion — extended models add others but those four are foundational.

items.q: What is a brand in marketing?
items.correct: A combination of name, design, symbols and associations that distinguishes a product and creates customer perception and loyalty — more than just a logo.
items.misconception: A brand is just the logo on a product.
items.random_wrong: A brand is the company that makes a product.
items.why: Branding is the sum of perceptions, not just visual identity. Coca-Cola the brand is much more than the script logo — it is associations, taste expectations, advertising history.

items.q: What did Mayo's Hawthorne studies suggest about motivation?
items.correct: Workers are motivated by social factors and the feeling of being noticed/valued — not just pay or physical conditions. Productivity rose because researchers paid attention to workers, not because lighting improved.
items.misconception: Mayo proved that better lighting increases productivity.
items.why: The Hawthorne effect is famously about attention and social meaning, not the experimental variable itself. Students remember the lighting experiment and miss the human relations conclusion Mayo drew from it.

items.q: A BOGOF offer is an example.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sales promotion. “Advertising” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Selling direct from a producer's website is.
items.why: Correct answer: Direct distribution. “Indirect distribution” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: An advert costs £4,000 and generates 200 extra sales at £25 profit each. What is the net gain, in £?
items.why: Exact answer: 1000. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
